smith_skate.name: Smithsonian National Museum of American History

items: Myth busted: patents, trademarks, and copyrights do not protect the same things.
Myth busted: not every U.S. patent has the same term; utility and design patents use different timelines.
Myth busted: buyer’s remorse does not automatically cancel every purchase; the FTC Cooling-Off Rule is limited.
Myth busted: a clever idea is not a complete business plan; customers, costs, financing, and execution still matter.
Myth busted: salesmanship does not permit any claim that closes a deal; advertising must be truthful and lawful.
Myth busted: animation is not continuous recorded motion; rapid still images create the illusion of movement.
Myth busted: a bugle’s sound begins mainly with the player’s vibrating lips, not the metal tube vibrating like a bell.
Myth busted: a rhetorical question is asked for effect, not because the speaker expects an answer.
Myth busted: news writing usually does not save the main point for the end; the inverted pyramid leads with key facts.
Myth busted: a color wheel does not prescribe one correct palette; it helps compare color relationships.
Myth busted: an aquifer is not usually a giant underground lake; it is permeable rock or sediment holding groundwater.
Myth busted: recovered paper is not simply washed and reused; it is repulped and manufactured into new products.
Myth busted: a textile’s environmental impact does not end at the factory; use, reuse, and disposal also matter.
Myth busted: an outdated building does not always need demolition; adaptive reuse can conserve materials and energy.
Myth busted: ecological overshoot is not merely one resource running low; total demand exceeds nature’s regeneration.
Myth busted: golf courses do not invent entirely separate games; the USGA and The R&A maintain shared rules.
Myth busted: feathering an oar does not add power; it rotates the blade nearly parallel during recovery.
Myth busted: kayak rocker is not decorative; the hull’s fore-and-aft curve affects maneuverability and tracking.
Myth busted: an ollie does not require grabbing the board; it is a foundational skateboard jump.
Myth busted: Smart Style is not only for experts; it teaches planning, awareness, and respect in terrain parks.
